vitals.date_specific: 2026: Sunday, September 6. Event HQ / rider-pack collection: Saturday, September 5, 10:00-19:00, Tweed Green, Peebles, EH45 8AP.
vitals.field_size: Approx. 2,200-2,500 riders (2,234 recorded in 2025; organizer-stated capacity up to 2,500)
vitals.entry_fee: GBP 84 standard (2026); GBP 89 with postal rider-pack delivery
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple feed stations with locally-made treats, water, and toilet stops

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 73. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 69.

course_description.character: A locally organized Scottish Borders sportive run by Hillside Outside Ltd out of Peebles, offering two routes: a 120 km / 1,200 m Full route and an 88 km / 940 m Challenge route. It's marketed and legally managed as a closed-road event, but that's not edge-to-edge — Moffat town centre runs as a neutralized, untimed open-road section on the Full route, agricultural and local-service vehicles may still operate around Talla and Megget on the Challenge route, and the final stretch back into Peebles uses traffic-management measures rather than a hard closure. Riders behind the 16 km/h minimum pace may continue on public roads once closures lift. The event paused for 2024 following local consultation, community objections, and closure difficulties, then returned in 2025 with 2,234 participants recorded in the council-commissioned economic impact assessment; that same assessment's post-event survey still found parking/access delays (11%) and food/drink queue or setup issues (12%) among riders' top improvement themes.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Wall of Talla on the Challenge route and the Devil's Beeftub climb and descent to Moffat on the Full route — run through genuinely quiet, scenic Borders country roads.
suffering_zones: Devil's Beeftub — a dramatic climb and descent to Moffat on the Full route, followed by the neutralized, untimed pass through Moffat town centre and on into Moffat Dale
Grey Mare's Tail and the St Mary's Loch stretch — exposed, rolling terrain late on the Full route where fatigue and any headwind compound
The Wall of Talla — a steep, punchy climb on the Challenge route near Talla Reservoir, the route's signature hard effort
Paddy Slacks — a late climb on the Full route arriving with legs already worked from the earlier Beeftub/Moffat sequence

logistics.lodging_strategy: Peebles and the Tweed Valley region offer accommodation options. Event HQ at Tweed Green, Peebles, EH45 8AP is open Saturday 5 September 10am-7pm for pre-event activities.

biased_opinion.verdict: Scenic Borders Terrain, Real Shared-Access and Local-Consultation History
biased_opinion.summary: Tour o' the Borders is a locally rooted sportive through some of Scotland's quieter Borders country, with two distance options and a charity tie-in to Sir Chris Hoy's Tour de 4. It's marketed as a closed-road event, but that claim needs a caveat: Moffat town centre runs open and neutralized on the Full route, and agricultural or local-service vehicles may still operate around Talla and Megget on the Challenge route — call it predominantly closed-road, not literally traffic-free. The event paused for 2024 following local consultation, community objections and closure difficulties, then returned in 2025 with 2,234 participants. The 2026 edition is confirmed and open for registration; the documented present-day frictions are narrower than the 2024 pause itself — the council-commissioned 2025 survey flagged parking/access delays (11%) and food/drink queue or setup issues (12%) as riders' top improvement themes.
strengths: Scenic, quiet Scottish Borders roads — Devil's Beeftub, Grey Mare's Tail, St Mary's Loch, and the Wall of Talla
Two-route structure (120 km Full / 88 km Challenge) giving riders a genuine choice of difficulty

Local organization (Hillside Outside Ltd) with a documented charity partnership (Tour de 4, benefiting Macmillan, Prostate Cancer UK, Maggie's, Breast Cancer Now, Cure Leukaemia in 2026) and a community fund (Touro Trust)
Well-attended 2025 return (2,234 riders) after the 2024 pause, per the council-commissioned economic impact assessment
weaknesses: Paused entirely for 2024 following local consultation, community objections, and closure difficulties — a real one-year gap in the event's history worth knowing about

'Closed-road' marketing overstates the reality: Moffat town centre is untimed/open, and agricultural or local-service vehicles may still operate around Talla and Megget — treat it as predominantly closed-road with exceptions, not fully traffic-free
Riders falling behind the 16 km/h minimum average may end up finishing on public roads after closures have lifted
The council-commissioned 2025 post-event survey recorded parking/access delays (11%) and food/drink queue or setup issues (12%) as riders' leading complaints
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A scenic, well-organized Borders sportive worth entering for the terrain and the charity tie-in — go in knowing it's 'predominantly' rather than fully closed-road, and that the event paused for a full year in 2024 over local road-closure consultation before returning in 2025.

final_verdict.one_liner: Genuinely scenic Scottish Borders sportive, predominantly (not fully) closed-road, that paused for a year in 2024 over local closure consultation.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes for the terrain and the two route options — the 2026 edition is confirmed and open for registration, though the 2024 pause is worth knowing as recent history.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a similarly scenic, community-organized closed-road sportive elsewhere in the UK, look at Etape Loch Ness. For a similarly scaled Irish sportive with a comparable community feel, see the Škoda Ring of Clare Sportive (also in this database). For a bigger-field Scottish road event, consider the Étape Caledonia.

eligibility.notes: 2026 edition confirmed for Sunday, September 6, 2026, via live organizer page and active SiEntries registration listing. Event paused for 2024 following local consultation, community objections, and closure difficulties (organizer's own account plus road.cc, 14 Jun 2023); returned successfully in 2025 (2,234 participants per Scottish Borders Council's 2025 economic impact assessment). Not literally 'fully closed roads' as previously stated in this profile's final_verdict — Moffat town centre runs open/neutralized and agricultural/local-service vehicles may still operate around Talla/Megget; corrected to 'predominantly closed-road.' Verified 2026-07-24 via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research, editorial wave 4.
